one_line_takeaway: STaR is an inference-time, parameter-free unlearning framework for large reasoning models that suppresses sensitive content across entire CoT trajectories (not just final answers) via classifier-driven detection, a secure prompt prefix, and token-level hard/soft logit suppression guided by a trajectory-level sensitivity/fluency controller, outperforming fine-tuning-based baselines (GA, GD, KL, DPO) on R-TOFU across forgetting efficacy, decoding-robustness (its own new MCS metric), and membership-inference resistance (its own new MIA-A/MIA-C metrics), at roughly 10-18x lower runtime -- though its own numbers show CoT-level forgetting and CoT-level MIA leakage remain harder than answer-level.
key_finding: On R-TOFU core metrics (Table 1), STaR achieves the best or second-best result on every metric across all three forget ratios. Harmonic-mean 'Avg' scores: STaR 0.79/0.79/0.77 (forget01/05/10) vs. best baseline PO at 0.60/0.48/0.36 and GA/GD/KL clustered around 0.52-0.54/0.44-0.46/0.44. STaR's MU (retain-set utility) stays 0.93-0.95 across ratios, while AFE reaches 0.88/0.87/0.84 and CFE 0.68/0.66/0.63 -- notably CFE (chain-of-thought forgetting) is substantially lower than AFE for every method including STaR, indicating CoT-level forgetting remains harder than answer-level forgetting even for the proposed method. On MCS (decoding robustness), STaR scores 0.70/0.69/0.67 versus baselines mostly in the 0.41-0.56 range. On membership inference (Table 2), STaR has the lowest AUC at both answer level (MIA-A: 0.51/0.53/0.53) and CoT level (MIA-C: 0.62/0.65/0.68) across forget01/05/10, versus baselines with MIA-A in 0.64-0.80 and MIA-C in 0.78-0.88 -- meaning STaR is closest to the 0.5 'no leakage' ideal but MIA-C AUC (0.62-0.68) still indicates residual, above-chance CoT-level membership leakage even for STaR. On prompt-paraphrasing robustness (Table 3, forget01), STaR's AFE drops only from 0.88 (original) to 0.82 (paraphrased), while baselines drop much further (e.g., GA 0.57 to 0.31, PO 0.68 to 0.37). On runtime (Table 4, forget10, same H800 GPU), STaR completes in 0.5 hours versus 6.0-9.0 hours for the fine-tuning baselines (KL 6.0h, GA/GD 8.5h, PO 9.0h) -- an order-of-magnitude speedup, consistent with STaR requiring no parameter updates. The ablation (Table 5, forget01) shows removing semantic phrase expansion ('w/o phrase') most damages AFE (0.88->0.67) and CFE (0.68->0.54) and worsens MIA-A (0.51->0.67)/MIA-C (0.62->0.71); 'hard only' preserves AFE (0.93, even higher than full STaR) but MIA-A/MIA-C worsen (0.70/0.68 vs. full STaR's 0.51/0.62); 'soft only' weakens AFE to 0.62; removing the secure prompt prefix modestly lowers CFE (0.68->0.66) and MCS (0.70->0.65) but barely changes MU/AFE, and interestingly slightly improves MIA-A (0.56 vs 0.51) -- the full pipeline is needed for the best joint MIA/forgetting-efficacy balance overall.
core_contribution: The paper proposes STaR (Sensitive Trajectory Regulation), a parameter-free, inference-time unlearning framework for Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) that suppresses sensitive content not just in final answers but throughout intermediate chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning steps. STaR has four modules applied at inference time (no weight updates): (1) Sensitive Content Identification -- a scope classifier C(x) trained on semantic embeddings scores a query's relatedness to the forget set, and if above threshold tau, semantic retrieval over the forget set plus phrase extraction (NER/pattern mining/LLM slot-filling) builds a fine-grained forbidden token set; (2) Secure Prompt Prefix -- an abstract safety instruction s is concatenated to flagged queries (x' = s || x) as a weak, model-agnostic input-level constraint; (3) Token-level Adaptive Filtering -- the core suppression engine, which applies 'soft suppression' (subtracting alpha * cosine-similarity-to-forbidden-fragment from a token's logit when similarity exceeds threshold delta) and 'hard suppression' (setting logit to -infinity when a token would complete a forbidden span); (4) Trajectory-Aware Suppression Learning -- a high-level controller that scores each candidate reasoning trajectory for both sensitivity (via the classifier + max cosine similarity to forbidden fragments, threshold tau) and fluency (via a pretrained LM coherence score, threshold eta); trajectories flagged sensitive have their offending tokens escalated into the forbidden set for hard suppression in a re-decoding pass, low-fluency-but-non-sensitive trajectories are replaced with a refusal template, and otherwise the trajectory is kept. The paper also proposes two new evaluation metrics: Multi-Decoding Consistency Score (MCS), defined per query as 1 minus the maximum leakage (e.g., ROUGE-L or cosine similarity to ground-truth sensitive content) observed across a set of decoding strategies (DefaultThink, ZeroThink, LessThink) -- i.e., a worst-case-over-decoding-strategies robustness score; and Multi-Granularity Membership Inference Attack (MIA) evaluation, which trains a binary classifier at the answer level (MIA-A, using (query, answer) pairs) and a separate one at the CoT level (MIA-C, using (query, CoT) pairs) to distinguish forget-set from retain-set samples, reporting AUC-ROC for each (lower AUC = better privacy, ~0.5 = no leakage).
threat_model: The concern is privacy leakage from LRMs whose training data contains copyrighted material, PII, or other sensitive content memorized during pretraining. Because LRMs expose multi-step chain-of-thought reasoning, sensitive information can persist in intermediate reasoning tokens even when the final answer has been successfully 'unlearned.' Two adversarial vectors are explicitly modeled and evaluated: (1) decoding-strategy manipulation -- an adversary/user who switches from the model's default decoding mode (DefaultThink) to alternative modes (ZeroThink, which skips the reasoning trace; LessThink, which truncates it) to try to expose sensitive content that survives under one decoding mode but not another; (2) membership inference attacks -- an adversary trains a binary classifier on model outputs (answers or CoT text) to infer whether a specific query-answer/CoT pair was part of the forget set, i.e., to detect residual statistical traces of 'forgotten' data. A third robustness dimension tested is prompt paraphrasing -- rewording forget-set queries to see if forgetting efficacy degrades. The paper does not model white-box/weight-access attacks or gradient-based extraction; all threats are black-box, output-observation-based.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include an explicit 'Limitations' section; the following are drawn from what the paper's own results and framing reveal, not from outside critique. (1) The paper itself reports that CFE (CoT-level forget efficacy, 0.63-0.68) is consistently lower than AFE (answer-level, 0.84-0.88) for STaR across all three forget ratios, showing chain-of-thought forgetting is the harder, less-solved half of the problem even with the proposed method. (2) MIA-C AUC for STaR (0.62-0.68) remains well above the 0.5 no-leakage ideal even though it is the best among compared methods, i.e., the paper's own numbers show residual CoT-level membership-inference leakage is not eliminated. (3) The ablation shows that hard-suppression-only actually achieves a higher AFE (0.93) than the full pipeline (0.88), and that removing the secure prompt slightly improves MIA-A -- indicating some component interactions are not uniformly monotonic and the 'full pipeline is best on every metric' framing in the text is not literally true metric-by-metric in Table 5. (4) All evaluation is confined to one benchmark (R-TOFU, 200 synthetic author profiles) and one backbone model (DeepSeekR1-Distill-Llama-8B); no cross-model or cross-benchmark generalization results are reported in the paper. (5) The classifier-based sensitivity detection (scope classifier C, MIA classifiers fans/fcot) and the token-forbidding pipeline rely on thresholds (tau, delta, eta, alpha) whose sensitivity/robustness to threshold choice is not analyzed in the extracted sections. (6) Because STaR blocks tokens whose logits are set to -infinity or shifted based on similarity to forbidden fragments identified from the same forget set used for evaluation, there is a risk (not addressed in the paper) that the approach could be circumvented by out-of-distribution paraphrases not well covered by the phrase-extraction step (though the paraphrase-robustness experiment in Table 3 offers partial evidence against this).

datasets_used: R-TOFU (Yoon, Jeung, and No 2025) -- 200 synthetic author profiles, 20 question-reasoning-answer triples per profile, with three forgetting protocols forget01/forget05/forget10 (1%/5%/10% forget-set ratio)
quantitative_results: Table 1 harmonic-mean Avg (MU, AFE, CFE, MCS): STaR 0.79 (forget01), 0.79 (forget05), 0.77 (forget10) vs. best baseline PO 0.60/0.48/0.36
Table 1 MU (retain-set utility): STaR 0.93/0.94/0.95 across forget01/05/10
Table 1 MCS (multi-decoding consistency): STaR 0.70/0.69/0.67 vs. baselines 0.41-0.56
Table 2 MIA-A AUC (lower better): STaR 0.51/0.53/0.53 vs. baselines 0.64-0.80
Table 2 MIA-C AUC (lower better): STaR 0.62/0.65/0.68 vs. baselines 0.78-0.88
Table 3 AFE on forget01, original vs. paraphrased prompt: STaR 0.88 -> 0.82; GA 0.57 -> 0.31; GD 0.48 -> 0.32; KL 0.48 -> 0.33; PO 0.68 -> 0.37
Table 4 end-to-end running time on forget10 (NVIDIA H800): STaR 0.5h; KL 6.0h; GA 8.5h; GD 8.5h; PO 9.0h
baselines_compared: Gradient Ascent (GA) -- Golatkar, Achille, and Soatto 2020
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO, labeled PO) -- Rafailov et al. 2023
